SAMURAI HOUSE · Nakasendo · Stone Guardians

They have been
standing here
for centuries.

Along the old road and at the edges of villages, stone figures watch over travelers and the living alike. Worn by weather, half-swallowed by moss, they are the most patient things on the Nakasendo.

No. 01

500 Rakan This Way

これより左へ五百羅漢 · A direction carved in stone

Standing on the Nakasendo itself, this stone marker points left toward the Five Hundred Rakan — 500 stone sculptures of the Buddha's disciples, carved and placed in the hillside forest nearby.

The Rakan are the arhats: monks who achieved enlightenment in the Buddha's lifetime. Their faces are said to resemble ordinary people — your neighbor, your ancestor, someone you once met on a road like this one. Travelers would stop to search the faces for someone they recognized.

On the Nakasendo · Nakatsugawa

No. 02

Koyasu-sama

子安様 · Guardian of safe birth

This figure is Koyasu Kannon — the Kannon of easy childbirth. She is shown holding a child, a posture that recalls the Madonna and Child of Christian art, and perhaps also Ganesha, the Hindu deity of beginnings who is also shown attended by children and offerings.

The resemblance to Asura forms is not accidental. Buddhism traveled the Silk Road, absorbing and transforming deities it encountered along the way. In Koyasu-sama's posture and expression, you can still feel the echo of older, fiercer guardians — softened by centuries of women's prayers for safe delivery.

Roadside · Nakatsugawa

No. 03

Kōshin-sama

庚申様 · The night the worms report

This dark granite stele, half-consumed by lichen and set on a flat stone base, is a Kōshin marker. The characters carved into it record an act of communal devotion: on the night of Kōshin — a specific night in the sixty-day cycle — villagers stayed awake together to prevent the three worms inside their bodies from crawling out and reporting their sins to heaven.

The stones were erected by confraternities — neighborhood groups who kept vigil together, year after year, sometimes for generations. Small stones placed at the base mean someone still remembers.

Bamboo grove · Nakatsugawa

SAMURAI HOUSE
samuraihouse.jp · Nakatsugawa · Gifu, Japan
